original_hypothesis.title: Direct theft of assets from connectors by abusing ConnectorMock2 as an enabled connector (if mistakenly deployed/allowed in prod)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ConnectorMock2 exposes sendTokensToTrustedAddress with no access control and can transfer arbitrary tokens to msg.sender. If this mock contract is ever enabled as a connector in a real vault (deployment/config mistake), an attacker can call it to pull any tokens it holds. Additionally, its addLiquidity and registry mutation helpers can corrupt holdings registry state if enabled.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Mocks frequently leak into production deployments. This mock has multiple public/external methods that bypass the trust model and can be abused immediately if it is whitelisted/enabled.
deployment_assumptions: ConnectorMock2 is deployed and added/enabled as a connector for a production vault (or used as a route/handler target)
Assets are ever moved into ConnectorMock2 (via transferPositionToAnotherConnector, mistaken transfers, or strategy operations)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: deployMockConnector.ts and updatePositions.ts show scripts intended to deploy and interact with ConnectorMock2; no guard preventing its deployment/use outside test environments is present in code.

proof_of_signal.pattern: external sendTokensToTrustedAddress transfers token to msg.sender with no auth

preconditions: ConnectorMock2 is enabled in Registry as an active connector for the vault
ConnectorMock2 holds valuable tokens (directly or via strategy move)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Mock has unrestricted token transfer and registry mutation helpers.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires misconfiguration (enabling mock) but then fully on-chain exploit.

operational_questions: Is ConnectorMock2 deployed on any target chain and enabled in any vault?
Do monitoring/allowlist processes prevent mock connectors from being enabled?
Can assets be routed into the mock via existing manager flows?
validation_requirements: Scan Registry connector lists for ConnectorMock2 bytecode hash
Attempt to call mock.sendTokensToTrustedAddress to drain tokens in a test setup
Review deployment pipelines for environment separation between test/prod

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Detect vault where ConnectorMock2 is enabled
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for or induce funds to be present in mock (or target any existing balance)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call sendTokensToTrustedAddress(token, amount, ..., data) to pull funds
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Optionally corrupt holdings registry via updatePositionToRegistryUsingType to create operational chaos

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Loss of connector-held assets if routed into mock
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Registry holding positions can be spammed/corrupted
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium-High; disable connector and reconcile registry state
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: TVL calculation, deposit/withdraw pricing, and strategy accounting

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Presence of ConnectorMock2 code; enabled connector entries pointing to it
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Direct transfers out of ConnectorMock2 to unknown EOAs

validation_result.mechanism: missing access control on mock connector token transfer
validation_result.impact_summary: ConnectorMock2.sendTokensToTrustedAddress has zero access control and transfers tokens to any msg.sender. If this mock is enabled as a connector and holds assets, anyone can drain them. Registry mutation functions can also corrupt holding position state if the mock is an enabled connector.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: ConnectorMock2.sendTokensToTrustedAddress is an external function with no access control modifiers or internal authorization checks. It unconditionally transfers tokens to msg.sender. This contrasts directly with BaseConnector.sendTokensToTrustedAddress which verifies the caller is the accounting manager, an active connector, the flashLoan address, or a valid swap route. The deployment script deployMockConnector.ts deploys ConnectorMock2 and adds it to the vault's connector list, and updateRegistry.ts calls addConnector to enable connectors. No environment guard prevents this from happening on a production network. The registry mutation functions (updatePositionToRegistryUsingType, addPositionToRegistryUsingType, addPositionToRegistry) also lack access control at the ConnectorMock2 level, though Registry.updateHoldingPosition itself checks if msg.sender is an enabled connector, providing a secondary guard for registry writes. However, sendTokensToTrustedAddress has NO such secondary guard - it directly transfers tokens.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: ConnectorMock2.sol lines 26-36 clearly show sendTokensToTrustedAddress with no access checks. BaseConnector.sol sendTokensToTrustedAddress (lines ~100-130) shows the proper authorization pattern with checks for accountingManager, isAnActiveConnector, flashLoan, and swapHandler.verifyRoute. The mock omits ALL of these checks. deployMockConnector.ts deploys the mock and adds it to vaultAddresses[chainId]['connectors']. updateRegistry.ts has updateConnectorsToRegistry that calls registry.addConnector, enabling connectors for a vault.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No access control. Any address can call this function and receive tokens held by ConnectorMock2. Compare to BaseConnector.sendTokensToTrustedAddress which checks msg.sender against accountingManager, active connectors, flashLoan, and swap routes.

No access control at the ConnectorMock2 level. While Registry.updateHoldingPosition checks if msg.sender (ConnectorMock2) is an enabled connector, any external caller can trigger this function if ConnectorMock2 is enabled, allowing arbitrary holding position manipulation.

No access control. Unlike BaseConnector.addLiquidity which checks registry.isAddressTrusted(vaultId, msg.sender), ConnectorMock2 allows any caller to invoke addLiquidity and pull tokens from the caller via the callback.

calling_sequence: 3. IERC20(token).safeTransfer(msg.sender, amount) executes - sends tokens to attacker
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The attack is trivially simple. ConnectorMock2.sendTokensToTrustedAddress at line 26-36 has no modifier and no internal authorization check. When called with empty data bytes, it executes IERC20(token).safeTransfer(msg.sender, amount) at line 31, sending the specified token amount to the caller. The only prerequisite is that ConnectorMock2 holds the requested token balance. Tokens can arrive in ConnectorMock2 via: (a) addLiquidity calls from the AccountingManager.executeDeposit flow if ConnectorMock2 is the target connector, (b) transferPositionToAnotherConnector from BaseConnector moving tokens into ConnectorMock2, or (c) direct ERC20 transfers.

deployment_assumptions_checked: ConnectorMock2 is deployed and added/enabled as a connector for a production vault
Assets are ever moved into ConnectorMock2
config_validation.deployment_evidence: deployMockConnector.ts deploys ConnectorMock2 with `const MockConnector = await ethers.getContractFactory('ConnectorMock2'); const mockConnector = await MockConnector.deploy(registryAddress, vaultId);` for vaultId '5656' and pushes it to vaultAddresses[chainId]['connectors']. updateRegistry.ts has `updateConnectorsToRegistry(['0xb70Cec752eAc08Ca604075Ac43CC65A72A2509E3'], '5656')` which calls `contract.addConnector(vaultId, address, address.map(() => true))`. No environment check (e.g., chainId == hardhat network) prevents deployment to production chains. The scripts use `process.env` RPC URLs for real networks (base, optimism, arbitrum, etc.).

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: If ConnectorMock2 is deployed and enabled as a connector for any vault, and tokens are transferred into it (via executeDeposit routing, transferPositionToAnotherConnector, or any other mechanism), any EOA can call sendTokensToTrustedAddress to drain those tokens. The attack requires: (1) ConnectorMock2 is enabled in Registry for a vault, (2) ConnectorMock2 holds tokens. The deployment scripts show both of these are operationally achievable. The registry mutation functions additionally allow any caller to add/remove/update holding positions through ConnectorMock2 if it is an enabled connector, potentially corrupting TVL calculations.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If exploited: direct theft of all tokens held by ConnectorMock2. If registry mutation functions are abused: corruption of holding positions affecting TVL calculation, share pricing, deposit/withdrawal amounts, and fee calculations. The severity is CRITICAL if ConnectorMock2 is enabled and funded in production, but the probability depends on deployment practices which are not verifiable from the code alone.
recommended_mitigations: Remove ConnectorMock2 from the production codebase entirely, or move it to a test-only directory excluded from deployment artifacts
Add environment guards in deployment scripts to prevent mock contract deployment on production chain IDs
Add a naming convention check or bytecode verification in Registry.addConnector to reject known mock contracts
If the mock must exist in the codebase, add NoyaGovernanceBase inheritance with proper access control modifiers matching BaseConnector's authorization pattern
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Registry, AccountingManager, and ConnectorMock2 in a Foundry test. 2. Add vault with ConnectorMock2 as an enabled connector. 3. Transfer ERC20 tokens to ConnectorMock2 (simulating executeDeposit routing). 4. From an attacker EOA, call ConnectorMock2.sendTokensToTrustedAddress(token, balance, attacker, '') and verify tokens are received. 5. From an attacker EOA, call registry mutation functions through ConnectorMock2 and verify holding positions are modified.
uncertainties: Whether vault ID 5656 in the deployment scripts represents a test or production vault
Whether the team has separate deployment pipelines for test vs production that are not visible in the provided scripts
Whether any off-chain monitoring would detect ConnectorMock2 deployment and alert operators
additional_testing_needed: Scan on-chain Registry connector lists on all deployed chains for ConnectorMock2 bytecode
Verify if any production vault has ConnectorMock2 enabled
Check if ConnectorMock2 holds any token balances on any chain
Review CI/CD pipeline for environment separation between test and production deployments
